text.title: AI Failure Mode Taxonomy Core Rules
text.summary: Six testable rules for a closed cross-team LLM failure-mode taxonomy.

rule.statement: Taxonomy contains exactly 12 modes. Adding a 13th mode is a versioned event, not a casual edit.
rule.rationale: Closed vocabularies stay teachable; open ones drift to a soup of synonyms within a year.

rule.statement: Every mode has a detector — a concrete machine or human signal that fires when the mode occurs. Modes without detectors are aspirational and are not in the taxonomy.
rule.rationale: Without a detector the mode cannot be triggered, counted, or graded; it is decoration.

rule.statement: The taxonomy is versioned (semver). Each release carries a CHANGELOG row referencing the issue / postmortem that motivated the change.
rule.rationale: Unversioned taxonomies cannot be cited reliably in postmortems; the issue trail makes drift defensible.

rule.statement: Mode ids are stable URN-shaped slugs (e.g. `fm.hallucination.fabricated-api`). Renaming a mode requires a deprecation period of one quarter with both names accepted.
rule.rationale: Renames without deprecation break every dashboard, eval, and ticket referencing the old id.

rule.statement: Every mode links to ≥1 methodology that mitigates or evaluates it (linked_methodology slug). Modes without a mitigation path are tracked but not "actionable".
rule.rationale: The taxonomy must be a routing table: from a detector firing to a known mitigation methodology.

rule.statement: Quarterly review re-tags 5% of recent incidents against the taxonomy. Modes used in <5% of incidents over two quarters are candidates for retirement.
rule.rationale: Without periodic review the taxonomy ossifies around the year-1 incident landscape.

rule.statement: Preconditions hold → execute the procedure and produce taxonomy.json passing the validator.
Single team or no central owner → skip; ship a local glossary instead.
